questions.question: In the standard memory segmentation model, which direction does the stack grow?
options: Upward (toward higher addresses)
Downward (toward lower addresses)
It doesn't grow — it's fixed size
Both directions simultaneously
questions.explanation: The stack grows downward toward lower addresses. The heap grows upward. This is the model assumed in the course for all exams.

questions.question: Which memory segment stores local variables and return addresses?
options: Heap
Data/Globals
Code (Program Text)
Stack
questions.explanation: The stack stores local variables, function arguments, saved frame pointers, and return addresses. This is why buffer overflows on the stack can overwrite return addresses.

questions.question: Which of the following C functions is vulnerable to buffer overflow?
options: fgets()
gets()
strncpy()
gets_s()
questions.explanation: gets() reads input with no bounds checking — it will write past the buffer boundary. fgets(), strncpy(), and gets_s() all accept size parameters for bounds checking.

questions.question: In a stack smashing attack, what does the attacker overwrite?
options: The heap metadata
The global variables
The return address on the stack
The code segment
questions.explanation: Stack smashing overwrites the return address stored on the stack. When the function returns, it jumps to the attacker-controlled address instead of the legitimate caller.

questions.question: What is a NOP sled?
options: A technique to slow down program execution
A sequence of NOP instructions placed before shellcode to increase the chance of landing on it
A type of network attack
A memory allocation strategy
questions.explanation: A NOP sled is a sequence of NOP (no-operation) instructions placed before shellcode. If execution lands anywhere in the sled, it 'slides' down to the shellcode, making the attack more reliable.

questions.question: What does the NX bit (No Execute) defense do?
options: Prevents writing to the stack
Marks memory regions (stack/heap) as non-executable so injected code cannot run
Encrypts the stack contents
Randomizes memory addresses
questions.explanation: The NX bit marks stack and heap memory as non-executable. Even if an attacker injects shellcode via buffer overflow, the CPU refuses to execute code from those regions.

questions.question: How does Return-to-libc bypass the NX bit defense?
options: It disables the NX bit
It writes shellcode to the code segment
Instead of injecting code, it redirects execution to existing executable functions in libc (like system())
It uses a hardware exploit
questions.explanation: Return-to-libc doesn't inject new code — it reuses existing executable code in libc. Since libc is in an executable memory region, the NX bit doesn't prevent calling its functions.

questions.question: What is Return-Oriented Programming (ROP)?
options: A programming paradigm for secure software
A technique that chains together small instruction sequences ('gadgets') already present in the program
A way to return error codes from functions
A compiler optimization technique
questions.explanation: ROP chains together 'gadgets' — small sequences of existing instructions that end with a 'ret' instruction. By carefully arranging return addresses on the stack, attackers create arbitrary computation from existing code.

questions.question: What is a stack canary (stack guard)?
options: A hardware security module
A random value placed between local variables and the return address that is checked before returning
A type of antivirus software
An encryption key for the stack
questions.explanation: A stack canary is a random value placed on the stack between the buffer and the return address. Before a function returns, the canary is checked — if it's been modified (by a buffer overflow), the program terminates.

questions.question: What does ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization) do?
options: Encrypts memory contents
Marks memory as non-executable
Randomizes the memory addresses of stack, heap, and libraries on each execution
Adds bounds checking to all arrays
questions.explanation: ASLR randomizes memory layout each time a program runs, making it much harder for attackers to predict where their shellcode, return addresses, or gadgets will be in memory.

questions.question: What does CFI (Control Flow Integrity) protect against?
options: Only buffer overflows
Only SQL injection
Attacks that redirect execution to unintended code paths by enforcing a predetermined control flow graph
Only network-based attacks
questions.explanation: CFI enforces that program execution follows a predetermined Control Flow Graph (CFG). It embeds special codes at valid jump targets and checks them before every computed jump, preventing ROP and other control-flow hijacking attacks.

questions.question: CFI makes three key assumptions: NWC, NXD, and NNQ. What does NWC (No Write Code) mean?
options: Code cannot write to files
No one can write new code to the executable code segment at runtime
Coders must not write insecure functions
The stack is write-protected
questions.explanation: NWC (No Write Code) assumes the code segment is immutable at runtime — attackers cannot modify or inject new executable code. This is typically enforced by the NX bit and memory protections.

questions.question: The heap grows toward lower addresses (downward) and the stack grows toward higher addresses (upward).
questions.explanation: It's the opposite: the heap grows upward (toward higher addresses) and the stack grows downward (toward lower addresses).

questions.question: gets() is a safe function for reading user input in C.
questions.explanation: gets() is one of the most dangerous functions in C — it performs no bounds checking and will happily write past buffer boundaries. Use fgets() or gets_s() instead.

questions.question: A single byte overflow cannot corrupt a return address.
questions.explanation: Even a 1-byte overflow can corrupt the least significant byte (LSB) of the return address, potentially redirecting execution to attacker-controlled code. Off-by-one errors are dangerous.

questions.question: ROP gadgets are Turing-complete on standard x86 machines.
questions.explanation: Research has shown that sufficient ROP gadgets exist on standard x86 machines to perform arbitrary computation — they are Turing-complete. This makes ROP extremely powerful.

questions.question: Stack canaries are regenerated on every function call.
questions.explanation: Stack canaries are typically generated once per program execution and reused across function calls. This is a limitation — if the canary value is leaked, all canaries are compromised.

questions.question: Which memory segment is typically read-only and contains the program's compiled instructions?
options: Stack
Heap
Data/Globals
Code (Program Text)
questions.explanation: The Code (Program Text) segment contains the compiled instructions and is typically marked as read-only to prevent modification.

questions.question: A buffer is 1024 bytes, followed by 8 bytes of saved frame pointer, then the return address. How many bytes minimum must be written to start overwriting the return address?
options: 1024 bytes
1025 bytes
1032 bytes
1033 bytes
questions.explanation: You need to fill the 1024-byte buffer + 8-byte saved frame pointer = 1032 bytes, then the 1033rd byte starts overwriting the return address.

questions.question: An attacker cannot inject shellcode due to NX, cannot use return-to-libc due to ASLR, and cannot chain ROP gadgets due to CFI. What attack technique might still work?
options: Stack smashing with NOP sled
A 'mimicry attack' — crafting a payload that follows valid control flow paths in the CFG
Simply disabling all defenses
Attacking the heap instead
questions.explanation: CFI's limitation is 'mimicry attacks' — the attacker can craft payloads that follow valid paths in the control flow graph. If the valid paths happen to enable useful malicious behavior, CFI cannot distinguish this from legitimate execution.

questions.question: Explain the attack chain: buffer overflow → NOP sled → shellcode execution, and why NX breaks this chain.
questions.answer: The attacker overflows a stack buffer to overwrite the return address, pointing it to a NOP sled in the buffer. When the function returns, execution jumps to the NOP sled, slides to the shellcode, and executes it. NX (No Execute) breaks this chain by marking the stack as non-executable — even though the shellcode is in memory, the CPU refuses to execute instructions from the stack region.

questions.question: Why does x86's variable-length instruction encoding make ROP easier compared to fixed-length architectures like ARM?
questions.answer: In x86, instructions can be 1 to 15 bytes long, so you can find 'gadgets' by jumping into the MIDDLE of existing instructions, creating entirely new instruction sequences through misalignment. In fixed-length architectures like ARM (4 bytes per instruction), you can only jump to actual instruction boundaries, greatly reducing the available gadget set.

questions.question: Which of the following is a limitation of stack canaries?
options: They prevent all buffer overflows
They can be brute-forced, leaked via format string vulnerabilities, and only protect the return address
They slow the program by 50%
They require special hardware
questions.explanation: Stack canaries can be brute-forced (since they're reused per execution), leaked via format string vulnerabilities, and they only protect the return address — they don't prevent overwriting other local variables.

questions.question: Which defense randomizes memory addresses to make exploits less reliable?
options: Stack canaries
NX bit
ASLR
CFI
questions.explanation: ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization) randomizes the base addresses of the stack, heap, and libraries each time the program runs, making it harder to predict target addresses.

questions.question: A developer replaces gets() with fgets(buf, sizeof(buf), stdin) but writes fgets(buf, sizeof(buf)+1, stdin). What vulnerability remains?
options: No vulnerability — fgets is always safe
Off-by-one error — reading one byte too many
Full buffer overflow — fgets ignores the size
SQL injection
questions.explanation: Specifying sizeof(buf)+1 means fgets will read one byte more than the buffer can hold — an off-by-one error. Even one byte can corrupt the LSB of adjacent data like the return address.

questions.question: The saved frame pointer (FP/EBP) on the stack points to:
options: The next function to call
The current stack frame's base, used to restore the caller's frame on return
The heap
The global variables section
questions.explanation: The frame pointer points to the base of the current stack frame. When the function returns, the saved FP is restored so the caller's stack frame can be properly accessed.

questions.question: What are the three assumptions that CFI makes (NWC, NXD, NNQ)?
options: No Weak Crypto, No XML Data, No Network Queries
No Write Code, No Execute Data, No Non-control-data modification by attacker
No Web Connections, No External Dependencies, No Null Queries
No Write Cache, No Execute Directly, No Nested Queries
questions.explanation: NWC = No Write Code (code segment is immutable), NXD = No Execute Data (data regions aren't executable — basically NX bit), NNQ = No Non-control-data modification (attacker can't modify non-control data to achieve goals without control flow hijacking).

questions.question: Describe fuzzing and how it helps discover buffer overflow vulnerabilities. What are the three types of fuzzing?
questions.answer: Fuzzing is an automated testing technique that feeds random, malformed, or unexpected inputs to a program to trigger crashes or unexpected behavior. It helps find buffer overflows by generating inputs that exceed expected bounds. The three types are: white-box (with source code access, uses program analysis), black-box (no source code, purely random/mutational inputs), and gray-box (partial knowledge, often uses code coverage feedback).

questions.question: The Morris Worm of 1988 exploited a buffer overflow in which Unix service?
options: The HTTP server
The finger daemon
The SSH server
The DNS resolver
questions.explanation: The Morris Worm exploited a buffer overflow vulnerability in the finger daemon (fingerd) on Unix systems, infecting approximately 6,000 computers — one of the first major internet worms.

questions.question: What is symbolic execution used for in security?
options: Encrypting program inputs
Determining which inputs cause specific code paths to execute, enabling systematic vulnerability discovery
Compiling programs faster
Managing memory allocation
questions.explanation: Symbolic execution treats inputs as symbols rather than concrete values and determines what input values cause each code path to execute. This enables systematic exploration of program behavior to find vulnerabilities.

questions.question: Concolic execution combines:
options: Encryption and hashing
Concrete execution and symbolic execution
Compilation and interpretation
Static analysis and fuzzing
questions.explanation: Concolic (concrete + symbolic) execution runs the program with concrete inputs while simultaneously maintaining symbolic constraints. This combines the practicality of concrete execution with the coverage analysis of symbolic execution.

questions.question: What is the Intel-specific name for the NX bit?
options: EVP (Enhanced Virus Protection)
XD (Execute Disable)
XN (Execute Never)
DEP (Data Execution Prevention)
questions.explanation: Intel calls it XD (Execute Disable). AMD calls it EVP (Enhanced Virus Protection). ARM calls it XN (Execute Never). Windows calls it DEP. Linux calls it ESP. OpenBSD calls it W^X.

questions.question: ASLR can be bypassed if an attacker can leak a base address through a format string vulnerability.
questions.explanation: ASLR's protection relies on address unpredictability. If an attacker can leak any address (e.g., via format string vulnerability), they can calculate the base address and defeat ASLR for that execution.

questions.question: In the context of CFI, what is a 'mimicry attack'?
options: An attack that mimics legitimate user behavior
An attack that disguises malware as legitimate software
An attack that crafts a payload following valid paths in the Control Flow Graph, making malicious behavior indistinguishable from legitimate control flow
An attack that copies another attacker's techniques
questions.explanation: A mimicry attack exploits CFI's fundamental limitation: if valid control flow paths in the CFG happen to enable malicious behavior, CFI cannot distinguish this from legitimate execution because the payload follows permitted paths.

questions.question: You're reviewing a C program and find: char buf[256]; strcpy(buf, argv[1]); — Explain the vulnerability, describe a possible exploit, and recommend a fix.
questions.answer: Vulnerability: strcpy() copies argv[1] into buf with no bounds checking. If argv[1] is longer than 255 bytes (+null), it overflows buf and overwrites adjacent stack data including the return address. Exploit: Attacker provides a crafted argument with 256+ bytes ending with a controlled return address pointing to shellcode or a ROP chain. Fix: Use strncpy(buf, argv[1], sizeof(buf)-1); buf[sizeof(buf)-1] = '\0'; to enforce bounds.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a vendor-specific name for the NX bit?
options: DEP (Data Execution Prevention) — Windows
W^X (Write XOR Execute) — OpenBSD
CFI (Control Flow Integrity)
XN (Execute Never) — ARM
questions.explanation: CFI is a separate defense mechanism that enforces control flow integrity. DEP, W^X, and XN are all vendor-specific implementations of the NX bit concept.

questions.question: An attacker finds that ASLR on a 32-bit system provides only 16 bits of randomization for the stack base address. If they can attempt the exploit 65,536 times before the program crashes permanently, what is their chance of success?
options: Nearly 0%
Approximately 100% — they can brute-force all 2^16 = 65,536 possible addresses
Exactly 50%
It depends on the payload size
questions.explanation: With 16 bits of entropy (2^16 = 65,536 possibilities) and 65,536 attempts, the attacker can try every possible address. This demonstrates why 32-bit ASLR provides relatively weak protection — the address space is too small.

questions.question: Which of the following is a safe alternative to gets() in C?
options: scanf("%s", buf)
strcpy(buf, input)
fgets(buf, sizeof(buf), stdin)
sprintf(buf, "%s", input)
questions.explanation: fgets() accepts a size parameter and will not read beyond it, making it a safe alternative to gets(). scanf("%s"), strcpy(), and sprintf() all lack bounds checking.

questions.question: OpenBSD's implementation of the NX bit concept is called:
options: DEP
XD
W^X (Write XOR Execute)
ESP
questions.explanation: OpenBSD calls it W^X (Write XOR Execute) — memory can be either writable or executable, but never both. This elegantly captures the core principle of the NX defense.

questions.question: What happens during a function's 'prologue' on the stack?
options: The return value is computed
Local variables are freed
The old frame pointer is saved, the frame pointer is updated, and space is allocated for local variables
The function's code is loaded into memory
questions.explanation: The function prologue: (1) pushes the old frame pointer onto the stack, (2) sets the frame pointer to the current stack pointer, and (3) decrements the stack pointer to allocate space for local variables.

questions.question: A format string vulnerability allows printf(user_input) instead of printf("%s", user_input). How can this bypass stack canaries?
options: It can't — format strings don't affect canaries
The attacker can use %x to read stack values, leaking the canary value, then craft an overflow that preserves it
It overwrites the canary with a null byte
It disables the canary check entirely
questions.explanation: Format string vulnerabilities can read arbitrary stack values using %x or %p. By leaking the canary value, the attacker can craft a buffer overflow that writes the same canary value in the correct position, bypassing the check.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'guards' in symbolic execution?
options: They protect against buffer overflows
They are conditions that control which code paths are executed — symbolic execution solves for inputs that satisfy these guards
They encrypt the program inputs
They monitor memory access
questions.explanation: Guards are conditional expressions (like if-statements) that determine which code paths are taken. Symbolic execution collects these guards and uses constraint solvers to find concrete inputs that satisfy specific path conditions.

questions.question: Compare and contrast NX bit, stack canaries, and ASLR as buffer overflow defenses. What does each protect against, and what is each one's main limitation?
questions.answer: NX bit: Prevents execution of injected code on stack/heap. Limitation: bypassed by return-to-libc and ROP (which reuse existing code). Stack canaries: Detect stack smashing by checking a sentinel value before function return. Limitation: value can be leaked (format strings) or brute-forced, and only protects the return address. ASLR: Randomizes memory layout to make address prediction harder. Limitation: can be defeated by leaking addresses or brute-force on 32-bit systems with low entropy.

questions.question: Return-to-libc attacks inject new executable code into memory.
questions.explanation: Return-to-libc attacks do NOT inject new code. They redirect execution to EXISTING library functions (like system() in libc) that are already in executable memory regions, bypassing NX.

questions.question: What does NX bit primarily mitigate?
options: execution of injected stack/heap code
All memory bugs permanently
Only SQL injection
Only social engineering
questions.explanation: NX bit mainly mitigates execution of injected stack/heap code.

questions.question: Which is a realistic limitation of NX bit?
options: code-reuse attacks such as ROP/ret2libc
No bypass exists in practice
It automatically repairs vulnerable code
It removes need for input validation
questions.explanation: Known limitation: code-reuse attacks such as ROP/ret2libc.

questions.question: NX bit alone provides complete protection against all memory corruption exploitation paths.
questions.explanation: False. NX bit improves security but must be layered with other controls.

questions.question: What does Stack canary primarily mitigate?
options: simple return-address overwrite attempts
All memory bugs permanently
Only SQL injection
Only social engineering
questions.explanation: Stack canary mainly mitigates simple return-address overwrite attempts.

questions.question: Which is a realistic limitation of Stack canary?
options: canary leaks/brute force and non-RA corruption
No bypass exists in practice
It automatically repairs vulnerable code
It removes need for input validation
questions.explanation: Known limitation: canary leaks/brute force and non-RA corruption.

questions.question: Stack canary alone provides complete protection against all memory corruption exploitation paths.
questions.explanation: False. Stack canary improves security but must be layered with other controls.

questions.question: What does ASLR primarily mitigate?
options: predictable absolute address targeting
All memory bugs permanently
Only SQL injection
Only social engineering
questions.explanation: ASLR mainly mitigates predictable absolute address targeting.

questions.question: Which is a realistic limitation of ASLR?
options: address leaks or brute force under low entropy
No bypass exists in practice
It automatically repairs vulnerable code
It removes need for input validation
questions.explanation: Known limitation: address leaks or brute force under low entropy.

questions.question: ASLR alone provides complete protection against all memory corruption exploitation paths.
questions.explanation: False. ASLR improves security but must be layered with other controls.

questions.question: What does CFI primarily mitigate?
options: jumps to invalid control-flow targets
All memory bugs permanently
Only SQL injection
Only social engineering
questions.explanation: CFI mainly mitigates jumps to invalid control-flow targets.

questions.question: Which is a realistic limitation of CFI?
options: mimicry attacks that remain on valid CFG paths
No bypass exists in practice
It automatically repairs vulnerable code
It removes need for input validation
questions.explanation: Known limitation: mimicry attacks that remain on valid CFG paths.

questions.question: CFI alone provides complete protection against all memory corruption exploitation paths.
questions.explanation: False. CFI improves security but must be layered with other controls.

questions.question: Unbounded input copy writes attacker data into a stack buffer Which diagnosis best explains this behavior?
options: Classic stack overflow risk from missing bounds checks.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Classic stack overflow risk from missing bounds checks.

questions.question: Unbounded input copy writes attacker data into a stack buffer Which immediate mitigation should be prioritized?
options: Adopt bounded APIs and strict length checks.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Adopt bounded APIs and strict length checks.

questions.question: A format-string bug leaks stack values before payload delivery. What is the most plausible technical diagnosis?
options: Leaked canary/address information can weaken layered defenses.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Leaked canary/address information can weaken layered defenses.

questions.question: A format-string bug leaks stack values before payload delivery. What is the strongest first containment/hardening step?
options: Fix formatting usage and sanitize outputs.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Fix formatting usage and sanitize outputs.

questions.question: Crash appears only when payload length crosses an exact boundary. Which diagnosis best explains this behavior?
options: Likely overwrite reaches adjacent control data.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Likely overwrite reaches adjacent control data.

questions.question: Crash appears only when payload length crosses an exact boundary. Which immediate mitigation should be prioritized?
options: Fuzz around boundaries and verify frame assumptions.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Fuzz around boundaries and verify frame assumptions.

questions.question: Service restarts allow repeated guessing in a low-entropy address space. What is the likeliest root technical cause?
options: Brute-force bypass risk against weak ASLR conditions.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Brute-force bypass risk against weak ASLR conditions.

questions.question: Service restarts allow repeated guessing in a low-entropy address space. Pick the best first defensive action:
options: Increase entropy and throttle retries/crashes.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Increase entropy and throttle retries/crashes.

questions.question: No shellcode is injected, but command execution still occurs Which diagnosis best explains this behavior?
options: Code-reuse attack (ROP/ret2libc) is likely.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Code-reuse attack (ROP/ret2libc) is likely.

questions.question: No shellcode is injected, but command execution still occurs Which immediate mitigation should be prioritized?
options: Combine CFI with hardening and gadget-surface reduction.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Combine CFI with hardening and gadget-surface reduction.

questions.question: User-controlled size field drives memcpy length and destination offsets. What is the most plausible technical diagnosis?
options: Out-of-bounds write may corrupt control or non-control data.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Out-of-bounds write may corrupt control or non-control data.

questions.question: User-controlled size field drives memcpy length and destination offsets. What is the strongest first containment/hardening step?
options: Validate size/offset invariants before memory operations.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Validate size/offset invariants before memory operations.

questions.question: Heap allocator metadata corruption follows malformed inputs. Which diagnosis best explains this behavior?
options: Heap exploitation path may yield arbitrary writes/reads.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Heap exploitation path may yield arbitrary writes/reads.

questions.question: Heap allocator metadata corruption follows malformed inputs. Which immediate mitigation should be prioritized?
options: Use hardened allocators and strict parser bounds checks.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Use hardened allocators and strict parser bounds checks.

questions.question: Unit tests pass but fuzzing reveals random parser crashes. What is the likeliest root technical cause?
options: Adversarial edge cases remain unhandled.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Adversarial edge cases remain unhandled.

questions.question: Unit tests pass but fuzzing reveals random parser crashes. Pick the best first defensive action:
options: Integrate continuous fuzzing with sanitizer feedback.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Integrate continuous fuzzing with sanitizer feedback.

questions.question: Symbolic execution finds a hidden path where constraints bypass checks Which diagnosis best explains this behavior?
options: Path constraints expose latent unsafe states.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Path constraints expose latent unsafe states.

questions.question: Symbolic execution finds a hidden path where constraints bypass checks Which immediate mitigation should be prioritized?
options: Enforce preconditions and state invariants.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Enforce preconditions and state invariants.

questions.question: Concolic tests reveal malformed packets flipping auth flags. What is the most plausible technical diagnosis?
options: Possible non-control-data corruption affecting logic.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Possible non-control-data corruption affecting logic.

questions.question: Concolic tests reveal malformed packets flipping auth flags. What is the strongest first containment/hardening step?
options: Protect critical state and validate transitions defensively.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Protect critical state and validate transitions defensively.

questions.question: Pointer values appear in production logs and metrics. Which diagnosis best explains this behavior?
options: Address disclosure can undermine ASLR assumptions.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Address disclosure can undermine ASLR assumptions.

questions.question: Pointer values appear in production logs and metrics. Which immediate mitigation should be prioritized?
options: Redact pointer-like values from logs/telemetry.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Redact pointer-like values from logs/telemetry.

questions.question: Legacy strcat calls repeatedly append into fixed buffers. What is the likeliest root technical cause?
options: Incremental concatenation can exceed capacity.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Incremental concatenation can exceed capacity.

questions.question: Legacy strcat calls repeatedly append into fixed buffers. Pick the best first defensive action:
options: Track remaining capacity and use bounded concatenation.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Track remaining capacity and use bounded concatenation.

questions.question: CFI is enabled, but a malicious sequence follows valid CFG edges Which diagnosis best explains this behavior?
options: Mimicry attack can remain within allowed structure.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Mimicry attack can remain within allowed structure.

questions.question: CFI is enabled, but a malicious sequence follows valid CFG edges Which immediate mitigation should be prioritized?
options: Add semantic policy checks beyond CFG structure.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Add semantic policy checks beyond CFG structure.

questions.question: Error-handling branch copies diagnostics into fixed arrays without clamps. What is the most plausible technical diagnosis?
options: Rare branch still contains exploitable memory path.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Rare branch still contains exploitable memory path.

questions.question: Error-handling branch copies diagnostics into fixed arrays without clamps. What is the strongest first containment/hardening step?
options: Test/fuzz failure paths and use shared safe-copy utilities.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Test/fuzz failure paths and use shared safe-copy utilities.

questions.question: Compiler warnings about truncation/format mismatch are ignored. Which diagnosis best explains this behavior?
options: Warnings can indicate real memory safety defects.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Warnings can indicate real memory safety defects.

questions.question: Compiler warnings about truncation/format mismatch are ignored. Which immediate mitigation should be prioritized?
options: Fail CI on high-confidence memory warnings.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Fail CI on high-confidence memory warnings.

questions.question: Parser trusts attacker metadata to allocate memory directly. What is the likeliest root technical cause?
options: Attacker-controlled metadata can trigger DoS/overflow paths.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Attacker-controlled metadata can trigger DoS/overflow paths.

questions.question: Parser trusts attacker metadata to allocate memory directly. Pick the best first defensive action:
options: Sanity-check metadata before allocation and processing.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Sanity-check metadata before allocation and processing.

questions.question: Canary is checked only on return while critical flags remain unguarded Which diagnosis best explains this behavior?
options: Non-RA corruption can alter behavior before canary check.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Non-RA corruption can alter behavior before canary check.

questions.question: Canary is checked only on return while critical flags remain unguarded Which immediate mitigation should be prioritized?
options: Separate critical flags and add integrity assertions.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Separate critical flags and add integrity assertions.

questions.question: Binary enables stack protector but omits PIE/ASLR-friendly hardening. What is the strongest first containment/hardening step?
options: Adopt comprehensive compiler/linker hardening profile.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Adopt comprehensive compiler/linker hardening profile.

questions.question: A hot-path optimization removes bounds checks. Which diagnosis best explains this behavior?
options: Performance tradeoff reintroduces exploitability.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Performance tradeoff reintroduces exploitability.

questions.question: A hot-path optimization removes bounds checks. Which immediate mitigation should be prioritized?
options: Preserve checks and optimize non-security-critical paths.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Preserve checks and optimize non-security-critical paths.

questions.question: High-risk parser is migrated to a memory-safe language. What is the likeliest root technical cause?
options: Can reduce broad classes of memory corruption bugs.
Only a networking ACL issue
Only a UX consistency issue
Only a key rotation schedule issue
questions.explanation: Can reduce broad classes of memory corruption bugs.

questions.question: High-risk parser is migrated to a memory-safe language. Pick the best first defensive action:
options: Prioritize risky modules for safer-language rewrites.
Disable monitoring
Increase default privileges
Rely on hidden architecture details
questions.explanation: Prioritize risky modules for safer-language rewrites.

questions.question: Which fuzzing mode matches this description: has source/internal visibility to guide input generation?
options: white-box
manual-only mode
compliance mode
cryptographic mode
questions.explanation: has source/internal visibility to guide input generation describes white-box fuzzing.

questions.question: white-box fuzzing can still be valuable when complete internal details are unavailable.
questions.explanation: Correct. white-box fuzzing is useful under realistic constraints.

questions.question: Which fuzzing mode matches this description: treats target as opaque and learns from I/O behavior only?
options: black-box
manual-only mode
compliance mode
cryptographic mode
questions.explanation: treats target as opaque and learns from I/O behavior only describes black-box fuzzing.

questions.question: black-box fuzzing can still be valuable when complete internal details are unavailable.
questions.explanation: Correct. black-box fuzzing is useful under realistic constraints.

questions.question: Which fuzzing mode matches this description: uses partial feedback such as coverage to steer mutations?
options: gray-box
manual-only mode
compliance mode
cryptographic mode
questions.explanation: uses partial feedback such as coverage to steer mutations describes gray-box fuzzing.

questions.question: gray-box fuzzing can still be valuable when complete internal details are unavailable.
questions.explanation: Correct. gray-box fuzzing is useful under realistic constraints.

questions.question: Compare fuzzing, symbolic execution, and concolic execution for finding memory corruption vulnerabilities. When should they be combined?
questions.answer: Fuzzing gives broad, scalable discovery; symbolic execution explores constrained deep paths; concolic combines concrete execution with symbolic guidance. Combine them when coverage plateaus: fuzz for breadth, then symbolic/concolic for hard-to-reach branches.

questions.question: You inherit a legacy C network parser. Provide a 30-day memory-safety hardening plan.
questions.answer: Week 1: inventory unsafe operations and threat model. Week 2: replace unbounded copies and enforce strict length checks. Week 3: enable hardening flags/sanitizers and continuous fuzzing. Week 4: triage crashes, add regression tests, and enforce CI security gates.

questions.question: What is 'type safety' in programming languages?
options: The guarantee that all language-level values will only be used in appropriate ways
A way to protect against SQL injection
A method for encrypting variable names in code
A technique for preventing network attacks
questions.explanation: Type safety means all language-level values will only be used in appropriate ways. Most modern languages are type safe/strongly typed. C and C++ are notable exceptions.

questions.question: What is a 'stack smashing attack'?
options: An attack exploiting a buffer overflow to overwrite the return address on the stack, redirecting execution to arbitrary code
An attack that fills up disk space until the system crashes
An attack that intercepts network packets
An attack that breaks encryption keys through brute force
questions.explanation: A stack smashing attack exploits a buffer overflow vulnerability to overwrite the return address on the stack, allowing the attacker to redirect program execution to arbitrary (often malicious) code.

questions.question: What is 'NOP sledding' (also called NOP sliding or NOP ramping)?
options: Filling a buffer with NOP instructions to create a large landing area for the return address, increasing exploit success
A technique for optimizing CPU performance by removing unused instructions
A network attack that floods a server with empty packets
A debugging technique that steps through code one instruction at a time
questions.explanation: NOP sledding fills the buffer with No Operation (NOP) instructions before malicious code, creating a larger landing area. Even if the attacker can't pinpoint the exact address, landing anywhere in the NOP sled will slide execution to the payload.

questions.question: What is a 'return-to-libc' attack?
options: An attack that overwrites the return address to point to an existing C library function (like exec()) to execute commands without code on the stack
An attack that returns error codes from the standard library
An attack that reverts a program to an older version of libc
An attack that modifies the libc source code at compile time
questions.explanation: A return-to-libc attack overwrites the return address to point to a function in the C standard library (like exec()) and passes a malicious command as an argument, bypassing NX protections since the code being executed is already in executable memory.

questions.question: What does ROP stand for and what is it?
options: Return-Oriented Programming — chaining existing code snippets ('gadgets') ending with ret instructions to perform arbitrary operations
Remote Object Protocol — a way to access objects over a network
Runtime Optimization Pipeline — a compiler technique
Recursive Output Processing — a data transformation method
questions.explanation: ROP stands for Return-Oriented Programming — a technique that reuses existing code snippets (gadgets) in executable memory, each ending with a ret instruction, chained together to perform arbitrary operations without executing code on the stack.

questions.question: What is 'fuzzing' in the context of software security?
options: Using software to provide many kinds of inputs to a program and monitor for unexpected behavior to find vulnerabilities
A technique for obfuscating source code to prevent reverse engineering
A method for compressing data to save storage space
A way to generate random encryption keys
questions.explanation: Fuzzing is providing a program many kinds of inputs (corner cases, very long inputs, special characters) and monitoring for crashes or unexpected behavior to discover vulnerabilities like buffer overflows.

questions.question: What is 'symbolic execution'?
options: A program analysis technique that treats inputs as symbolic variables to determine what inputs cause each code block to execute
Running a program with symbols instead of numbers
A type of code obfuscation that replaces variable names with symbols
A debugging technique that visualizes program flow with symbols
questions.explanation: Symbolic execution treats program inputs as symbolic variables rather than concrete values, exploring all possible execution paths to determine what inputs cause each block to execute — useful for achieving high test coverage.

questions.question: What is 'concolic execution'?
options: A hybrid technique combining concrete execution (specific inputs) with symbolic execution to explore program paths more efficiently
Running two programs concurrently to compare their outputs
A type of code compilation that produces two different binaries
A network technique that routes traffic through two paths simultaneously
questions.explanation: Concolic execution (concrete + symbolic) combines running the program with specific inputs and symbolic analysis. It allows focusing on specific inputs of interest while still systematically exploring execution paths.

questions.question: What is a 'stack guard' (static canary)?
options: A random value placed before the return address that is checked before returning — if altered, a buffer overflow occurred
A physical lock on the server rack
A backup copy of the stack stored in a separate memory region
A firewall rule that protects stack-based services
questions.explanation: A stack guard places a small random value (canary) on the stack before the return address. When a function returns, it checks if the canary has been altered — if so, a buffer overflow is detected and the program terminates.

questions.question: What does ASLR stand for and what does it do?
options: Address Space Layout Randomization — randomizes memory segment locations each time a program runs to prevent address prediction
Automated Security Log Review — a log analysis tool
Application-Specific Logic Router — a network component
Advanced System Layer Recovery — a backup technique
questions.explanation: ASLR stands for Address Space Layout Randomization — it randomizes the memory addresses of code, data, and stack segments each execution, making it harder for attackers to predict where to jump.

questions.question: What does CFI stand for and what does CFG stand for?
options: Control Flow Integrity / Control Flow Graph — CFI ensures program control flow follows the predetermined CFG
Central Firewall Interface / Common Filter Gateway — network security tools
Code Format Inspector / Compiler Flag Generator — development tools
Cryptographic Function Interface / Certificate Format Guide — encryption standards
questions.explanation: CFI = Control Flow Integrity: ensures program control flow follows expected paths. CFG = Control Flow Graph: a directed graph of basic blocks and possible control transfers used by CFI to validate jumps.

questions.question: What is the NX bit (No Execute bit)?
options: A hardware feature that marks memory pages as non-executable, preventing code execution on the stack/heap
A software flag that disables network connections
A compiler option that removes unused code
A CPU feature that speeds up floating-point operations
questions.explanation: The NX (No Execute) bit is a hardware feature that marks memory pages as non-executable. When set on the stack/heap, the CPU raises an exception if code execution is attempted from those regions.

questions.question: What is a 'worm' in the context of malware?
options: A type of virus that can self-replicate and spread across networks without user interaction
A program that slowly corrupts files over time
A type of encryption that wraps data in multiple layers
A debugging tool that traces through code line by line
questions.explanation: A worm is a type of malware that can self-replicate and spread across computer networks without user interaction. Worms are a subset of viruses with the condition that they involve a network.

questions.question: What was the Morris Worm (1988) and why is it historically significant?
options: The first widespread buffer overflow attack — it exploited the finger daemon on Unix systems and infected ~6,000 computers
The first computer virus ever created — it infected floppy disks
The first ransomware attack — it encrypted files and demanded payment
The first DDoS attack — it overwhelmed early internet routers
questions.explanation: The Morris Worm (1988) was the first widespread buffer overflow attack, exploiting a 512-byte buffer overflow in the finger daemon on Unix systems. It infected ~6,000 computers and caused ~$10M in damages.

questions.question: What do FP/EBP and SP/ESP stand for in the context of stack frames?
options: Frame Pointer (Extended Base Pointer) and Stack Pointer (Extended Stack Pointer) — they manage the current stack frame
File Permission and System Process — operating system concepts
Function Parameter and Static Property — programming terms
Forward Proxy and Secure Protocol — networking concepts
questions.explanation: FP = Frame Pointer (EBP = Extended Base Pointer in x86): points to the beginning of the current frame. SP = Stack Pointer (ESP = Extended Stack Pointer in x86): points to the next available location on the stack.

questions.question: What do NWC, NXD, and NNQ stand for? These are assumptions for CFI.
options: No Write Code, No eXecute Data, No Non-control data modification — three assumptions required for CFI to work
Network Wide Configuration, Next eXtended Domain, Non-Nullable Query — database terms
New Worker Context, No XML Dependencies, Nested Name Qualifier — programming terms
Native Wireless Controller, Numeric eXchange Device, Named Network Queue — networking terms
questions.explanation: NWC = No Write Code (attacker can't write to code segment). NXD = No eXecute Data (attacker can't execute code from data segment). NNQ = No Non-control data modification that affects control flow. These are assumptions CFI relies on.

questions.question: Define 'stack guard' (canary), 'ASLR', and 'CFI'. For each, name one limitation.
questions.answer: Stack Guard: places a random canary value before the return address; checked on function return to detect buffer overflow. Limitation: attacker may guess/brute-force the canary or leak it via format string vulnerabilities. ASLR: randomizes memory segment locations each run to prevent address prediction. Limitation: if attacker leaks one address, all other addresses in that segment can be calculated. CFI: ensures program control flow follows the predetermined control flow graph (CFG). Limitation: 'mimicry' attacks where attacker stays on valid control flow paths but reaches unintended destinations.

questions.question: What is a format string in C/C++?
options: A string that formats output text with bold or italic styles
A string containing format specifiers (like %s, %d, %x) used in functions like printf to specify how arguments are formatted and displayed
A string that is automatically encrypted before being printed to the console
A fixed-length string that cannot be modified after initialization
questions.explanation: A format string contains format specifiers (such as %s, %d, %x, etc.) that are used in functions like printf to specify how the arguments should be formatted and displayed. They act as 'holes' that get filled via the extra arguments passed to the function.

questions.question: What does the varargs (variadic functions) feature in C/C++ allow?
options: Functions to accept only a fixed number of arguments determined at compile time
Functions to return multiple values simultaneously
Functions to accept a variable number of arguments, commonly used in printf and scanf
Functions to automatically validate all input parameters
questions.explanation: The varargs feature allows functions to accept a variable number of arguments. It is commonly used in functions like printf and scanf, where the number of arguments varies based on the format string. It is implemented using macros defined in the <stdarg.h> header.

questions.question: What does the %x format specifier do in a C printf() call?
options: Prints the argument as a decimal integer
Prints the argument as a hexadecimal number
Prints the argument as an octal number
Prints the argument as a string
questions.explanation: The %x format specifier tells printf to expect an integer argument and print it in hexadecimal format. For example, printf("%x", 91) would print '5b'.

questions.question: What does the %o format specifier do in a C printf() call?
options: Prints the argument as a hexadecimal number
Prints a literal percent sign
Prints the argument as an octal number
Prints the argument as a floating-point number
questions.explanation: The %o format specifier tells printf to expect an integer argument and print it in octal format. For example, printf("%o", 91) would print '133'.

questions.question: Given the C code: printf("%s %d %x %o %%\n", "c", 91, 91, 91); — what is the output?
options: s d x o %
questions.explanation: %s prints the string 'c', %d prints 91 in decimal, %x prints 91 in hexadecimal (5b), %o prints 91 in octal (133), and %% prints a literal percent sign.

questions.question: Why is calling printf(user_input) dangerous, compared to printf("%s", user_input)?
options: printf(user_input) is slower and uses more memory than printf("%s", user_input)
printf(user_input) cannot handle strings longer than 256 characters
If user_input contains format specifiers like %x or %n, the attacker can read from or write to arbitrary memory locations
printf(user_input) will automatically execute any shell commands embedded in the string
questions.explanation: When user input is passed directly as the format string, an attacker can embed format specifiers like %x (to read stack values) or %n (to write to memory). With printf("%s", user_input), the user input is treated as data, not as a format string, so specifiers are printed literally.

questions.question: In the context of format string vulnerabilities, what does the %n format specifier do?
options: Prints a newline character to the output
Prints the number of arguments remaining on the stack
Writes the number of bytes printed so far to the memory address pointed to by the corresponding argument
Reads the next N bytes from the input stream
questions.explanation: The %n specifier writes the number of bytes output so far to the address pointed to by the corresponding argument on the stack. In a format string attack, an attacker can use %n to write arbitrary values to arbitrary memory locations, making it particularly dangerous.

questions.question: Consider: sprintf(buffer, "Warning %10s -- %8s", message, uname); where buffer is 32 bytes. The %10s specifier represents a MINIMUM width of 10 characters. If uname is user-controlled and exceeds 8 characters, what vulnerability occurs?
options: A format string read vulnerability that leaks memory contents from the stack
A buffer overflow because %10s and %8s specify minimum (not maximum) widths, so longer inputs overflow the 32-byte buffer
A null pointer dereference because sprintf cannot handle inputs longer than the width specifier
An integer overflow in the width calculation that wraps the buffer size
questions.explanation: The width specifiers in format strings (like %10s and %8s) specify MINIMUM characters to print, not maximum. The designers of C intended this as a formatting feature, not a security boundary. If the combined output exceeds the 32-byte buffer, a buffer overflow occurs. This is a key insight: format string width specifiers do not limit input length.

questions.question: The width specifier in a C format string (e.g., %10s) sets a maximum number of characters that can be printed for that argument.
questions.explanation: The width specifier sets a MINIMUM, not a maximum. If the argument has more characters than the width, all characters are still printed. This design decision can lead to buffer overflows when used with functions like sprintf, because the output may exceed the destination buffer size.

questions.question: A format string vulnerability can be used both to read values from the stack and to write values to arbitrary memory locations.
questions.explanation: Format specifiers like %x and %p can be used to read values from the stack (information disclosure), while %n can be used to write values to memory addresses. This dual capability makes format string vulnerabilities especially dangerous.

questions.question: Variadic functions in C (like printf) automatically validate that the number and types of arguments match the format specifiers in the format string.
questions.explanation: Variadic functions in C do NOT automatically validate argument count or types against the format string. If more format specifiers exist than arguments provided, printf will read whatever values happen to be on the stack, which can lead to information disclosure or crashes.

questions.question: Name the five basic format specifiers discussed in class (%s, %d, %x, %o, %%) and briefly describe what each one does.
questions.answer: %s prints a string, %d prints a decimal integer, %x prints a hexadecimal integer, %o prints an octal integer, and %% prints a literal percent sign.

questions.question: An attacker controls the format string passed to printf(). They provide the input "%x %x %x %x". What information does this reveal?
options: The contents of the program's source code stored in the code segment
Sequential values from the stack in hexadecimal, potentially leaking sensitive data like canary values, return addresses, or local variables
The contents of environment variables stored in the heap memory region
The machine's IP address and network configuration details
questions.explanation: Each %x reads the next value from the stack and prints it in hexadecimal. Since printf uses the stack to access its variadic arguments, providing extra %x specifiers without corresponding arguments causes printf to read and display whatever values happen to be on the stack, potentially leaking canary values, return addresses, and other sensitive data.

questions.question: Why did the designers of C make the width specifier in format strings (e.g., %10s) represent a minimum rather than a maximum number of characters?
options: It was a deliberate security measure to prevent truncation of important output data
It was designed for text alignment and formatting purposes in an era when security attacks were not a primary design consideration
Maximum width enforcement was too computationally expensive for systems of that era
The C standard committee later reversed this decision, but legacy code retained the old behavior
questions.explanation: The width specifier was designed for text formatting and alignment purposes during 'different times' when people weren't designing for attacks. It ensures output is at least a certain width for neat column alignment, but this design means it provides no security boundary on input length.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct way to safely print user-controlled input using printf in C?
options: printf(user_input);
printf("%s", user_input);
printf(user_input, "%s");
printf("%" + user_input);
questions.explanation: printf("%s", user_input) treats user_input as data to be printed, not as a format string. The format string "%s" is a constant controlled by the programmer. In contrast, printf(user_input) uses the user's input as the format string itself, allowing format specifier injection.

questions.question: What C header defines the macros used to implement variadic functions (varargs)?
options: <stdlib.h>
<string.h>
<stdarg.h>
<stdio.h>
questions.explanation: The varargs feature is implemented using macros defined in the <stdarg.h> header, which provides macros like va_list, va_start, va_arg, and va_end to access the variable arguments passed to a variadic function.

questions.question: What is a 'variadic function' in C/C++?
options: A function that accepts a variable number of arguments, implemented using macros from <stdarg.h>
A function that can only accept exactly two arguments of different types
A function that automatically validates the types of all its arguments at runtime
A function whose return type changes depending on the number of arguments passed
questions.explanation: Variadic functions accept a variable number of arguments. In C/C++, they are implemented using the <stdarg.h> header with macros like va_start, va_arg, and va_end. Common examples include printf, scanf, and snprintf.

questions.question: In a format string vulnerability, why does printf(uname) where uname = "%s" cause a security issue?
options: It causes a segmentation fault because printf cannot process format specifiers without arguments
printf interprets %s as a format specifier, reading the next stack word as a char* and leaking sensitive data
It prints the literal string "%s" to the console output
The compiler rejects this code at compile time due to type mismatch errors
questions.explanation: When printf receives "%s" as its format string with no additional argument, it interprets %s as a format specifier and reads whatever is next on the stack, treating it as a char* and dereferencing it. This can leak sensitive stack data — a classic format string vulnerability.

questions.question: Consider: void f(char* u) { char s[512]; snprintf(s, sizeof(s), u); } with snprintf NOT inlined. If an attacker provides u = "%x%x", what two values from the stack are leaked?
options: The first two bytes of the source code segment and the program counter value
The function's oldFP (frame pointer) and a portion of buffer s, as hexadecimal values
Two copies of the return address stored in snprintf's own stack frame
The heap pointer and the size of the dynamically allocated memory region
questions.explanation: Since snprintf has no extra arguments beyond the format string, each %x consumes the next word on the stack walking upward into f's frame. In the non-inlined layout, these would be f's oldFP and a portion of s, leaking raw stack addresses in hexadecimal.

questions.question: In the format string attack using input "abcd%d%n" against snprintf with an inlined call, what does the %n specifier ultimately do?
options: It prints the number of characters written so far to the output buffer
It reads the next stack word as an int* and writes the total character count to that memory address
It terminates the string by inserting a null byte at the current position
It causes snprintf to return an error code indicating an invalid format
questions.explanation: The %n specifier does not print anything. Instead, it reads the next word from the stack, interprets it as an int*, and writes the number of characters printed so far to that address. This enables an arbitrary memory write primitive when the attacker controls the format string.

questions.question: In the "abcd%d%n" format string attack against an inlined snprintf, the bytes 'a','b','c','d' end up in s[0..3]. On a little-endian system, what address does %n write to?
options: 0x61626364 — the ASCII values in order from first to last
0x64636261 — the ASCII values reversed due to little-endian byte ordering
0x00006162 — only the first two bytes are used as a 16-bit pointer
0x64636261 — but the write fails because this is not a valid heap address
questions.explanation: On a little-endian system, the bytes 'a'(0x61), 'b'(0x62), 'c'(0x63), 'd'(0x64) stored at s[0..3] are reinterpreted as the 32-bit pointer 0x64636261. The %n specifier writes the total character count to this address. The attacker controls where to write by choosing the leading bytes of the input.

questions.question: In a %n-based format string attack, how does the attacker control the VALUE written to the target address?
options: By changing the bytes at the start of the format string to encode the desired value directly
By adjusting the number of characters printed before %n, for example using width specifiers like %100d to inflate the count
By passing the desired value as an additional argument after the format string
By repeating the %n specifier multiple times to increment the value at the target address
questions.explanation: The %n specifier writes the total number of characters printed so far. The attacker controls this count by padding the output — for example, %100d prints the integer with at least 100 characters of width, inflating the count without adding real data. The attacker controls WHERE by choosing the leading bytes (the pointer), and WHAT by adjusting the character count before %n.

questions.question: The %n format specifier in C gives an attacker an arbitrary memory write primitive when they control the format string, because they control both the target address (via the leading bytes) and the value written (via the character count).
questions.explanation: This is true. The %n specifier writes the number of characters printed so far to an address it reads from the stack. When the attacker controls the format string, they control the leading bytes (which become the target address) and can inflate the character count (which becomes the value written) using width specifiers.

questions.question: What is the single most important mitigation against format string vulnerabilities?
options: Enable ASLR on the operating system to randomize all memory layout addresses
Never use untrusted input as a format string — use fixed strings and pass data as arguments
Apply static canaries to every stack frame to immediately detect overwrites
Compile with optimization level -O3 to inline all function calls and reduce stack frames
questions.explanation: The most important rule is: never use untrusted input as a format string. Always use a fixed format string and pass user data as an argument (e.g., printf("%s", uname) instead of printf(uname)). This applies to all variadic functions, not just printf.

questions.question: Which GCC/Clang compiler flags help detect format string vulnerabilities at compile time?
options: -Wall -Werror
-Wformat -Wformat-security
-fstack-protector -fPIE
-O2 -march=native
questions.explanation: The -Wformat and -Wformat-security flags catch many format string issues at compile time. However, static analysis is fundamentally undecidable for this problem — it cannot catch all instances, especially when the format string is computed at runtime.

questions.question: Compiler flags like -Wformat-security can catch all possible format string vulnerabilities, including cases where the format string is computed at runtime.
questions.explanation: While -Wformat and -Wformat-security catch many cases at compile time, static analysis is fundamentally undecidable for this problem. It cannot catch all instances, especially when the format string is computed at runtime from dynamic data.

questions.question: When snprintf is inlined by the compiler into function f, how does the stack layout change compared to the non-inlined version, and why does this matter for format string attacks?
options: The inlined version uses the heap instead of the stack, making format string attacks impossible
snprintf's locals are merged into f's frame, so %s now consumes f's oldFP instead of snprintf's, potentially leaking the frame pointer of f's caller
The inlined version places a security canary between f's and snprintf's frames automatically
Inlining has no effect on the stack layout because the compiler preserves separate frames for security
questions.explanation: With inlining, snprintf's locals are merged into f's frame. The stack layout changes so that when %s consumes the next stack word, it now gets f's oldFP (which points to whoever called f) instead of snprintf's separate frame data. This leaks the frame pointer of f's caller, revealing memory layout information that can defeat ASLR.

questions.question: Explain the "abcd%d%n" format string attack step by step. What does each part of the input do, and why does it give the attacker an arbitrary memory write?
questions.answer: Step 1: 'abcd' is written into the buffer s[0..3]. Step 2: %d consumes the next stack word (oldFP), printing it as a decimal integer and advancing the character count and stack cursor. Step 3: %n consumes the next stack word, which is s[0..3] (the bytes 'abcd' = 0x64636261 on little-endian), interprets it as an int*, and writes the total character count to that address. The attacker controls WHERE by choosing the leading bytes (the target address) and WHAT by adjusting the character count before %n (e.g., using width specifiers like %100d).

questions.question: What is an 'integer overflow' in the context of secure coding?
options: When an arithmetic result exceeds the maximum value of a fixed-width type, causing it to wrap around
When a program allocates too many integer variables, exhausting available stack space for storage
When an integer is cast to a floating-point type and loses its original precision value
When a program attempts to divide an integer by zero, triggering a hardware exception signal
questions.explanation: An integer overflow occurs when an arithmetic operation produces a result that exceeds the maximum (or minimum, for underflow) value representable by the fixed-width type. On most systems, unsigned integers wrap modulo 2^n, which can lead to security vulnerabilities.

questions.question: Which of the following C/C++ types are considered 'integers' that can be subject to overflow vulnerabilities?
options: Only int and unsigned int, because they are the primary integer types in C programming
char, short, int, long, long long, size_t, ptrdiff_t, and pointers — all fixed-width numeric types
Only size_t and ptrdiff_t, because they are specifically used in memory operations
float and double as well, since they also use fixed-width IEEE 754 bit representations
questions.explanation: In C/C++, all fixed-width numeric types are subject to overflow: char, short, int, long, long long (signed and unsigned), size_t, ptrdiff_t, and even pointers (which are internally integer-width address values). float and double are NOT integers — they have their own precision/rounding issues under IEEE 754.

questions.question: Consider: void f(unsigned int len) { float *fa = (float*)malloc(len * sizeof(float)); fa[len-1] = 3.14; } On a 32-bit system where sizeof(float) = 4, what happens if len = 2^30 + 2?
options: malloc allocates exactly (2^30 + 2) * 4 bytes and the program works correctly
The multiplication wraps modulo 2^32 to just 8 bytes, malloc allocates a tiny buffer, and fa[len-1] writes astronomically out of bounds
malloc detects the overflow and returns NULL, preventing the vulnerability
The compiler automatically promotes the multiplication to 64-bit arithmetic, preventing the overflow
questions.explanation: len * sizeof(float) = (2^30 + 2) * 4 = 2^32 + 8. On a 32-bit system, size_t is 32 bits, so this wraps modulo 2^32 to just 8 (enough for 2 floats). Then fa[len-1] tries to write at index 1073741825 — astronomically out of bounds. This is a classic integer overflow vulnerability.

questions.question: On a 32-bit architecture, what is the type and range of size_t?
options: A signed 32-bit integer that can represent values from -2^31 to 2^31 - 1
An unsigned 32-bit integer that can represent values from 0 to 2^32 - 1
A 64-bit unsigned integer regardless of architecture
A compiler-dependent type with no guaranteed minimum size
questions.explanation: size_t is an unsigned integer type guaranteed to be wide enough to hold the size of any object. On a 32-bit architecture, size_t is 32 bits, so it can represent values from 0 to 2^32 - 1. This means arithmetic on size_t values wraps modulo 2^32.

questions.question: Floating-point types (float, double) are integers in C/C++ and are subject to the same overflow wrapping behavior as int or unsigned int.
questions.explanation: Floating-point types are NOT integers. They use IEEE 754 representation and have their own class of precision and rounding issues. They do not wrap modulo 2^n like integer types. However, they can still be security-relevant — for example, truncation when cast to an integer type.

questions.question: Which of the following is a recommended mitigation for integer overflow vulnerabilities?
options: Use ASLR to randomize where integer variables are stored in memory
Validate inputs before arithmetic and use safer numeric libraries like SafeInt or compiler built-ins like __builtin_mul_overflow
Compile with the -fno-strict-aliasing flag to prevent the compiler from optimizing integer operations
Always use floating-point types instead of integers for size calculations
questions.explanation: Key mitigations include: validating inputs before arithmetic, using safer numeric libraries (e.g., SafeInt in C++, compiler built-ins like __builtin_mul_overflow), using type-safe languages (Rust panics on overflow in debug mode), and employing static analysis tools like Coverity or PVS-Studio.

questions.question: How does Rust handle integer overflow differently from C/C++?
options: Rust uses arbitrary-precision integers by default, so overflow is impossible
Rust panics on overflow in debug mode and provides explicit wrapping/checked arithmetic in release mode
Rust converts all integers to floating-point before arithmetic to prevent wrapping
Rust uses the same wrapping behavior as C but logs a warning message at runtime
questions.explanation: Rust panics (crashes) on integer overflow in debug mode, immediately catching the bug. In release mode, Rust provides explicit wrapping arithmetic (e.g., wrapping_add) and checked arithmetic (e.g., checked_mul which returns None on overflow), giving developers control over overflow behavior.

questions.question: Static analysis tools can catch all integer overflow vulnerabilities in a program, especially those that depend on runtime input values.
questions.explanation: Static analysis tools like Coverity and PVS-Studio can detect some overflow patterns, but they cannot catch all of them — especially when the overflow depends on runtime input. This is a fundamental limitation of static analysis.

questions.question: In the integer overflow example with malloc(len * sizeof(float)) on a 32-bit system, why does the multiplication of len = 2^30 + 2 by sizeof(float) = 4 wrap to exactly 8?
options: Because 4 * 2 = 8, and the 4 * 2^30 term is discarded by the compiler optimizer
Because multiplying by 4 is a left-shift by 2 bits; the two high bits of len (from the 2^30 term) shift into bit 32 and are lost, leaving only the low bits (2 * 4 = 8)
Because malloc rounds down all allocations to the nearest power of 2
Because size_t on 32-bit systems is actually only 3 bytes (24 bits), so the maximum representable value is much smaller
questions.explanation: Multiplying by 4 (sizeof(float)) is equivalent to a left-shift by 2 bits. The value 2^30 in len has its significant bit at position 30; shifting left by 2 moves it to bit 32, which exceeds the 32-bit size_t range and is lost. Only the low bits remain: 2 * 4 = 8 bytes.

questions.question: Explain why all primitive data types in C/C++ require care from a secure coding perspective. Give at least two categories of types and describe the specific risks for each.
questions.answer: All fixed-width integer types (char, short, int, long, size_t, pointers) can overflow or underflow, wrapping modulo 2^n for unsigned types. This can lead to undersized buffer allocations, out-of-bounds writes, or bypassed security checks. Floating-point types (float, double) have their own class of precision and rounding issues under IEEE 754 — for example, truncation when cast to an integer can produce unexpected values. Both categories require careful input validation and bounds checking.

questions.question: In a format string attack using snprintf, an attacker uses %p %p as the format string with no extra arguments. What does %p print?
options: Rust uses arbitrary-precision integers by default, which prevents overflow from ever occurring
Rust panics on overflow in debug mode and provides explicit wrapping/checked arithmetic in release mode
Rust converts integers to floating-point before arithmetic operations to prevent wrapping behavior
Rust uses the same wrapping behavior as C/C++ but logs a warning message at runtime for debugging
questions.explanation: The %p specifier prints pointer values in the system's implementation-defined format (typically 0x-prefixed hex). Without corresponding arguments, it reads the next words from the stack and prints them as pointers, leaking oldFP and adjacent stack contents. Attackers use this to defeat ASLR.

questions.question: If a format string vulnerability is used to leak canary values, return addresses, or frame pointers, that information can then be used to defeat ASLR or craft ROP chain attacks.
questions.explanation: Attackers use format string techniques (%x, %p) to leak canary values, return addresses, or frame pointers from the stack. This leaked information can defeat ASLR (by revealing base addresses) and enable follow-up attacks such as ROP chains.

questions.question: What does the 'printf("ab%n", &i);' statement do in C?
options: Prints 'ab' and then prints the value of i
Prints 'ab' and then sets i = 2 (the number of characters printed so far)
Prints 'abn' because %n is not a real format specifier
Causes a compilation error because %n is deprecated in modern C
questions.explanation: printf("ab%n", &i) prints 'ab' (2 characters) and then %n writes the number of characters printed so far (2) into the variable i. So after this call, i = 2. The %n specifier is what makes format string attacks so dangerous — it enables memory writes.
